title: Build and Print a Dynamic 2D Array
prompt_md:
## Goal

Dynamically allocate a 2D integer array, fill it with values read from input, and print it row by row. Properly deallocate all memory.

## Input

- Line 1: two integers `rows` and `cols` (1 <= rows, cols <= 10)
- Next `rows` lines: `cols` space-separated integers per line

## Output

- Print the matrix row by row. Within each row, separate values with a single space. Print a newline after each row.

## Requirements

- Declare an `int**` and use `new` to allocate an array of `int*` pointers, then allocate each row with `new int[cols]`.
- After printing, deallocate each row with `delete[]`, then deallocate the array of pointers with `delete[]`.
- Do not use `std::vector` or any container class.

title: Transpose a Dynamic Matrix
prompt_md:
## Goal

Read a matrix, build its **transpose** in a second dynamically allocated 2D array, and print the transposed matrix. The transpose of an `R x C` matrix is a `C x R` matrix where element `[r][c]` becomes element `[c][r]`.

## Input

- Line 1: two integers `R` and `C` (1 <= R, C <= 10)
- Next `R` lines: `C` space-separated integers per line

## Output

- Print the transposed matrix (`C` rows, `R` columns). Within each row, separate values with a single space. Print a newline after each row.

## Requirements

- Allocate the original matrix as `int**` with `new`.
- Allocate a second `int**` for the transpose (dimensions `C x R`).
- Copy values so that `transposed[c][r] = original[r][c]`.
- After printing, deallocate both matrices completely (rows first, then the pointer arrays).
- Do not use `std::vector` or any container class.
